transcript:
You're watching hundreds of loose points drift in the dark — dust with nowhere to belong. Then, one by one, small clusters snap into closed outline shapes, and each time one does, it flashes bright before settling into a faint, glowing perimeter.

Here's the part that's easy to miss: those shapes never get filled in. Look at the code where a form gets created — the fill is explicitly set to "none." The interior isn't colored, isn't textured, isn't even the same void as the background — it's just... undefined until the edge draws it into being. Then watch what happens when a shape dissolves: in the function called "dissolve bounded form," the piece doesn't just erase it. It calls "record dissolution," leaving a warm afterimage that quietly increases the odds a new shape will form in that same spot later — so endings actually seed beginnings.

So the pulse you're seeing travel across the canvas isn't decoration — it's the moment particles stop being scattered potential and become a specific, bounded thing. Nothing was ever "inside" until the edge said so.
